nodes.label: Scan-Fix-Verify Three-Phase Workflow
nodes.rationale: Splitting the audit into Scan, Fix, and Verify phases ensures every fix is validated against the original violation via a baseline comparison (--baseline audit-baseline.json), preventing regressions instead of trusting one-pass fixes.

nodes.label: Severity Classification (Critical/Major/Minor)
nodes.rationale: Each severity tier maps to an SLA (Critical: fix before release; Major: current sprint; Minor: within 2 sprints) so remediation effort is prioritized by user impact rather than treated as a flat list.

nodes.label: ARIA Live Regions
nodes.rationale: The live region container must exist in the DOM before content is injected; adding aria-live to a newly created element will not announce it, which is why the pattern pre-renders empty announcement containers.

nodes.label: sr-only Screen Reader Utility Class
nodes.rationale: Visually hides content while keeping it in the accessibility tree, unlike display:none which removes it from screen readers; the focusable variant restores visibility on keyboard focus for skip links.

nodes.label: 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Level AA)
nodes.rationale: Authentication must not depend on cognitive function tests (remembering passwords, solving puzzles); the prescribed mitigations are password-manager support, passkeys/biometrics, never blocking paste, and OTP alternatives to CAPTCHA.

nodes.label: Aceternity shadcn Registry (@aceternity)
nodes.author: Claude Skills Maintainers
nodes.rationale: Components are distributed copy-paste style via a namespaced shadcn registry rather than an npm package, so full component source lands in the codebase and can be customized directly.

nodes.label: GPU-Accelerated Properties (transform/opacity only)
nodes.rationale: Animating transform and opacity stays on the compositor thread; animating left/top/width/height/margin triggers layout and paint, causing jank especially on low-end devices.

nodes.label: will-change (use sparingly)
nodes.rationale: Reserved only for complex animations and reset to auto afterwards, because leaving will-change active holds GPU layers and wastes memory.

nodes.label: Modern Web Design Component Templates Library
nodes.norm_label: modern web design component templates library

nodes.label: Astro + React Islands + Tailwind CSS v4 Stack
nodes.norm_label: astro + react islands + tailwind css v4 stack

nodes.label: Spelling Counts Rule (Listening)
nodes.rationale: Highlighted in the overview and repeated in Section 1 because, unlike Reading, a misspelled Listening answer is marked wrong; the transfer time is the designated moment to check spelling.

nodes.label: Location Language (Prepositional Phrases for Maps)
nodes.rationale: Presented as 'the whole game' for map tasks because answers are given relative to landmarks, so recognising relational phrases like 'opposite', 'next to', 'at the end of' is the core skill.

nodes.label: Distractors (The Distractor Game)
nodes.rationale: Section 3 speakers usually mention every answer option; only one answers the question asked, so the lesson trains listening for where opinions end up rather than where they start.

nodes.label: Simulation — Students Discussing a Research Project (Urban Heat Islands)
nodes.norm_label: simulation — students discussing a research project (urban heat islands)

nodes.label: The Three Reading Passages (Difficulty Progression)
nodes.rationale: Passages increase in difficulty from 1 to 3; the lesson recommends budgeting roughly 20 minutes per passage because easy marks in a later passage outweigh one stubborn question.

nodes.label: Reading Lesson — True / False / Not Given
nodes.norm_label: reading lesson — true / false / not given

nodes.label: True / False / Not Given (Question Type)
nodes.rationale: The key distinction taught: NOT GIVEN means the passage is silent on the statement, not that the statement is wrong.

nodes.label: Matching Headings (Question Type)
nodes.rationale: A heading is a title for the whole paragraph's main idea; one matching detail does not make a correct heading — the core trap the lesson warns against.

nodes.label: Matching Paragraph Information (Question Type)
nodes.rationale: Explicitly contrasted with Matching Headings: this type asks which paragraph contains a specific detail, not what a paragraph is mainly about.

nodes.label: Speaking Marking Criteria (Four Criteria, 25% Each)
nodes.rationale: All three speaking parts are scored against the same four equally-weighted criteria across the whole test, which is why every part lesson restates them.

nodes.label: Answer + Reason + Example/Detail Formula
nodes.rationale: Chosen as the Part 1 answer template because it naturally yields the recommended 2-4 sentence answer length — long enough to show language, short enough to stay conversational.

nodes.label: Speaking Part 2 — Cue Card / Long Turn Lesson
nodes.norm_label: speaking part 2 — cue card / long turn lesson

nodes.label: PEEL Method (Point, Explain, Elaborate, Link)
nodes.rationale: Used as a loose framework for the 2-minute talk so candidates cover all cue-card bullets while leaving room for personal reactions where strong vocabulary shines.

nodes.label: OREO Formula (Opinion, Reason, Example, Opinion)
nodes.rationale: Part 3 requires extended, reasoned answers (4-6+ sentences); OREO forces a stated position, justification, illustration, and a balanced restatement.

nodes.label: Collocation (Vocabulary Learning Principle)
nodes.rationale: The overview teaches recording collocations rather than isolated words because examiners reward precision and natural word partnerships, not rare words used awkwardly.

nodes.label: IELTS Writing Task 1
nodes.rationale: 20 minutes, 150+ words; Academic = report on a visual, General Training = letter. Deliberately allocated only a third of the Writing mark, so lessons steer candidates to protect Task 2 time.

nodes.label: IELTS Writing Task 2
nodes.rationale: 40 minutes, 250+ words, counts double vs Task 1 in the band formula (Task 1 + Task 2 x 2) / 3 - the site's advice to master the essay first follows from this weighting.

nodes.label: Four Writing Marking Criteria
nodes.rationale: Task Achievement/Response, Coherence & Cohesion, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range & Accuracy - weighted equally at 25% each, whole band per criterion, averaged. Used as the shared scoring frame across lessons and the Essay Checker.

nodes.label: Task Achievement
nodes.rationale: The criterion most often cited as a hard cap in the lessons: missing overview caps around Band 5, unclear position caps Task Response at Band 6, under-length answers are penalised.

nodes.label: Essay Checker
nodes.rationale: Site feature at ../writing/checker that scores essays on exactly the four official criteria using the public band descriptors, so feedback matches what examiners reward.

nodes.label: How to Answer Task 1 (Universal Method) Lesson
nodes.norm_label: how to answer task 1 (universal method) lesson

nodes.label: Four-Paragraph Task 1 Method
nodes.rationale: Introduction, Overview, Detail 1, Detail 2 - one structure for every Task 1 visual. Design intent: teach the method once in this lesson so each type lesson can focus only on what to look for in that visual, avoiding repetition across chart/process/map lessons. General Training letters are the deliberate exception with their own format.

nodes.label: Overview Paragraph
nodes.rationale: Called the most important paragraph: 2-3 key features, no numbers, starts with 'Overall,'. Without a clear overview Task Achievement is capped around Band 5, which is why every report-type lesson repeats it.

nodes.label: Charts, Graphs & Tables Lesson
nodes.rationale: Focuses on data analysis (change-over-time vs static comparison) rather than structure, because the four-paragraph method is owned by the method lesson.

nodes.label: Trend Language
nodes.rationale: Verb/noun pairs for rise, fall, plateau, fluctuation and extremes, graded with adverbs - the Lexical Resource toolkit specific to chart reports.

nodes.label: Passive Voice for Processes
nodes.rationale: Man-made processes take the passive ('the glass is crushed') because the doer is unimportant; natural processes stay active ('the water evaporates'). Process reports test grammar rather than data selection since there are usually no numbers.

nodes.label: Maps & Plans Lesson
nodes.rationale: Describe changes between two maps (added/removed/replaced/expanded) with past simple + present perfect and location language; never describe each map separately.

nodes.label: Letters Lesson (General Training)
nodes.rationale: The only Task 1 type with its own format instead of the four-paragraph report: greeting, purpose opening, one paragraph per bullet point, closing line, sign-off - no overview paragraph.

nodes.label: Three Letter Tones
nodes.rationale: Formal / semi-formal / informal, each pairing a greeting with its sign-off. Consistency matters more than fanciness: one mixed-register phrase drops the Task Achievement band.

nodes.label: Opinion Essays Lesson
nodes.rationale: Most common Task 2 type ('to what extent do you agree or disagree'). Golden rule: position clear in the introduction and consistent to the end; one well-defended side beats a wobbly balance because clarity of argument, not ideas, is graded.

nodes.label: TEE Paragraph Structure
nodes.rationale: Topic sentence -> Explanation -> Example: the body-paragraph shape used for each supporting reason in Task 2 essays.

nodes.label: Discussion Essays Lesson
nodes.rationale: 'Discuss both views and give your own opinion' = three compulsory jobs: explain view A fairly, explain view B fairly, make your own position explicit. One-sided essays lose Task Response marks.

nodes.label: Voice-Signalling Language
nodes.rationale: Phrases attributing each sentence to view A, view B or the writer ('proponents argue' / 'critics point out' / 'in my view'). Without them the examiner cannot tell reported views from the writer's own, and an unclear position caps Task Response at Band 6.

nodes.label: Problem & Solution Essays Lesson
nodes.rationale: Read the question forensically: 'causes and solutions' is a different essay from 'problems and solutions'.

nodes.label: Matching Principle
nodes.rationale: Every solution must answer a problem the essay raised, with a named actor (governments, employers, schools, individuals) and a brief evaluation; two developed problem-solution pairs beat five thin ones.

nodes.label: Two-Part Questions Lesson
nodes.rationale: One question per body paragraph is called the safest Task 2 structure: it keeps Coherence clean and makes it impossible for the examiner to think half the task was forgotten. The second question often smuggles in an opinion requirement.

nodes.label: Essay Grader Worker
nodes.rationale: Cloudflare Worker proxy so the static site never sees the Gemini API key: the site POSTs the essay, the Worker calls Gemini and returns the assessment JSON. Rejects essays over 20k chars or under ~20 words, and surfaces Gemini 429 as 'daily free grading limit reached'. CORS only restricts browsers - direct curl abuse is accepted as a trade-off for a free-tier study tool, with Cloudflare rate limiting or Turnstile as the escalation path.

nodes.label: Gemini Flash (free tier)
nodes.rationale: Chosen because the free tier (~1,500 requests/day, no credit card) is enough for a study tool; default model gemini-2.5-flash via GEMINI_MODEL var. Trade-off: free-tier Gemini may use submitted student essays for model improvement.

nodes.label: PUBLIC_GRADER_URL Variable
nodes.rationale: Env/Actions variable pointing the site at the deployed Worker URL; set locally in .env and in GitHub Actions repo variables for production builds.

nodes.label: Offline Sample Grader Fallback
nodes.rationale: With PUBLIC_GRADER_URL unset the site quietly falls back to the offline sample grader - graceful degradation so nothing breaks without the Worker.

nodes.label: ALLOWED_ORIGINS CORS Config
nodes.rationale: Comma-separated origins allowed by CORS (wrangler.jsonc vars); must be extended if the site moves to a custom domain. Only protects against browser use, not direct curl.

nodes.label: Update IELTS Materials Workflow
nodes.rationale: SOP for scraping fresh practice questions and examiner tips from trusted IELTS sources into each lesson's Practice & Resources section. Checks robots.txt per source and skips disallowed domains; failed sources are skipped with a warning, leaving existing materials untouched. Since the Astro migration (July 2026), targets src/content/lesson-bodies/<slug>.html fragments and changes go live on push via GitHub Actions.

nodes.label: scrape_ielts_materials.py
nodes.rationale: Deterministic tool (requests + beautifulsoup4) that fetches source URLs with a browser User-Agent, extracts tips/practice questions, and rebuilds the Practice & Resources card section in each lesson fragment.

nodes.label: LESSON_SOURCES Config
nodes.rationale: Dict at the top of scrape_ielts_materials.py mapping each lesson fragment to its source URLs; the single place to edit when a source URL moves or a new lesson is added.

nodes.label: MATERIALS Comment Markers
nodes.rationale: <!-- MATERIALS --> ... <!-- /MATERIALS --> markers in each lesson fragment delimit the injected region, so scraped content can be replaced idempotently without touching hand-written lesson content; also the manual-edit escape hatch when a source is blocked.

nodes.label: IELTS Liz (source site)
nodes.rationale: Trusted source for examiner tips across Reading, Writing T1/T2, Speaking P1-P3, Listening and Vocabulary lessons.

nodes.label: IELTS Up (source site)
nodes.rationale: Trusted source for practice questions across all lesson pages.
